evidence_quotes.quote: You can't tell me that they don't make a mountain money off of advertising and personal data sales, even with a tiny few bypassing ads and restrictions.
locking a device owned by an individual so that they can't choose what they do with it

paragraphs.text: A large part of the world is in a very dangerous state right now. The policing of owned devices and yes, even piracy is continuing to squeeze the chokehold on the general public. It is tantamount to utter stupidity to start policing software and internet at this stage. Big names such as Google, Microsoft, and Apple think they have market monopolies, when in reality its been in the comfort of freedom of choice that has kept competitors at bay. The moment digital media and software becomes locked down is the moment that a major gap is presented to competitors. I've already seen people looking at Fairphone, Linux phone alternatives, etc. We don't care about their higher end technology. We care about freedom and alternatives, and there's a large amount of us. The Linux community is growing at a never before seen rate as Microsoft and Apple squeeze their customers harder and harder with each update. Privacy is taking front stage, and that's not where big corporations want it to be. They've forgotten their place as quiet surveillance and think they've gotten so big that no one can stop them. The monetary vacuum that they are is now apparent. They care nothing for their customers, they just want to make sure you're using their pre-approved cash-cows at every turn. Its unwise. Software should have a certain amount of freedom. Most of us FOSS users don't bypass security or advertising. Most of us find cool timers or habit trackers to use, or maybe an alternative calendar app that doesn't upload our loved one's birthdays to a digital database. I understand that advertisement bypassing is a problem, but locking a device owned by an individual so that they can't choose what they do with it is the same as smashing a rose with a hammer because it had a spider on it. You can't tell me that they don't make a mountain money off of advertising and personal data sales, even with a tiny few bypassing ads and restrictions. People are going to become loud. They're going to become restless and angry. The more they police media, the more we'll become unsatisfied. Putting shackles on the majority just to catch a tiny few just shows ineptitude, greed, and is a huge misuse of power. It won't go unnoticed. Big tech and digital corporations would be smart to stay quiet and allow a complicated freedom. The more they cry out against the few, the more the ignorant masses begin to see whats happening and start to realize they too could be utilizing alternatives. Calling it "sideloading" is pretty much admitting that people don't know that they can install alternative software. They use a code word like they think people are too stupid to look deeper into the meaning. Its not only a wild abuse of power, its a direct insult to the civilian people. Plain and simple.
